_note: ORIGINAL content. Think C++ and the Google C++ Class outline informed coverage only; no prose, examples, or exercises were copied.
title: Pointers
summary: Understand addresses before you follow them.
refresher: A pointer stores the address of another value. `&value` gets the address. `*pointer` follows the address to reach the value. `nullptr` means the pointer points to nothing, so check before using `*pointer`.

blocks.body: Every variable lives at some spot in memory. A pointer stores that spot. It does not store a second copy of the value. It stores directions to the original value.

In `int* pointer = &score;`, `&score` means "the address of score." Later, `*pointer` means "go to that address and use the value there." The star has two jobs: it helps declare a pointer, and it helps follow one.

blocks.title: Python usually hides this layer
blocks.body: Python keeps this layer mostly hidden. C++ lets you work with addresses directly. That gives you control, but it also means you must check where a pointer points before using it.

blocks.caption: The pointer stores score's address. Assigning through `*pointer` therefore changes score itself.
Check for a valid target before following an address.

blocks.body: `nullptr` means there is no target. Following it is like walking through a door that is not there. Check first, then use `*pointer` only when the pointer has a real address.

blocks.title: Never follow an unknown address
blocks.body: Initialize a pointer when you create it. Use `nullptr` when it has no target, then check before using `*pointer`. Dereferencing a null or uninitialized pointer is invalid.

blocks.why: The `&` operator gets x's memory address, and the pointer stores that address.

blocks.prompt: What does `*p` mean in an expression?
choices: the value at the pointed address
blocks.why: Dereference follows the stored address and accesses the object located there.

blocks.title: Pointers checklist
items: Use nullptr when there is no target.
Use a reference when "nothing" is not allowed.
